speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor is Jack Rosenthal. You were talking about the march in the snow. Please continue with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, we were marched out to Buchenwald. And it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Marched out from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Auschwitz. I'm sorry. Marched out from Auschwitz. And as we were going, you know, the snow piled up because I had wooden-- leather shoes. And when the-- when you have a lump on your foot, and it's wood, so you start going like a duck. And it was OK. You could clean it up sometimes. But what happened, people didn't have enough strength. And after a while, they would just sit down. Once you sat down in the-- everybody passed through. In the-- from behind, there was a-- there was German soldiers. They would shoot you. Once you stayed behind, they kill you. As a matter of fact, in my transport, I personally didn't see it, but somebody told me and I remember this, that one German sat down, a German soldier, took his rifle, put it in his mouth and pulled the trigger. He killed himself. Yeah, he killed himself. Apparently he was a good Nazi. I want you to know what happened to me up to this point, and I'm talking 1945, is nothing compared to what's going to happen now. That night-- the following day-- we marched all night, all night, all night. The following day we marched and one night we came to a place called Nikolaev. And there was a big, big farm over there. And there was one, one, one house. One building, like a barn or something. And I went into the barn. And everybody tried to get into that barn, but there was no room.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the barn, they had some beams across. So I was young. I climbed up and I sat on the beam. So [INAUDIBLE], whoever wanted to go on the beam, sat on the beam. It was like a beam across.

speaker: interviewee
text: An SS officer walks in. He had a sub machine gun, shoots down everybody from the beam, just like this. When I saw him-- he started on this end. I, I, I, I jumped off. You know, I fell down on the floor. In the morning, I got up and I knew people that were in main camp. And I saw people standing outside. I remember man with stubbles. The stubbles were white, like frozen. But the men was staying there. They looked like alive. And there was one guy, his name was Pincus. And I walked over to him. I said, Pincus, I said to him, and I just gave him a little push and he turned over. They froze to death standing up. They were-- they looked, they looked alive. Well, we stayed there over, over that night, and then they start pushing us. And we went to a place called Gleiwitz. Gleiwitz was the first German town, which was on the Polish-German border. No, no. We went through a town called Katowice. From Nikolaev, we went to a town called Kattowitz. Today, it's called Katowice. Katowice today is the capital of Silesia, of lower Silesia. It's a big city today. There the coal mines are, the coal, the coal area. Very rich in minerals. And I went-- when I went through it, it was a small, a small little town. And I-- when I went through Katowice, we went through the, the center of town. And I see children and civilians inside the building, because nobody was on the streets, because when Jews-- when we arrived to a station, they would make an announcement. [GERMAN] You know, a Jew. So everybody ran away, because the Jews were like, you know, like, like, a [NON-ENGLISH], like, like, like, like a plague was coming. So everybody-- you know. They were all inside the-- inside the houses. And I saw children. I saw civilians. So it brought me back a little memories, you know, from, from, from civilian life. We went to Katowice. And then we came into a town called Gleiwitz. Gleiwitz was already in the-- see, Kattowitz was on the Polish side-- at one time, not today. Before the War, Kattowitz was on the Polish side. Gleiwitz was on the German side. And we were in Gleiwitz. And then they brought in trains. No, they took us out to the rail station there, to a railroad station. And there were trains. But the trains, they were not enclosed trains. They were open. They were open trains. They were not cattle cars, they were like freight cars, open, halfway open. And they put us-- they pushed the thing into those cattle cars. And the trains started to move. And the train moved to Prague. I remember we passed through Prague towards Buchenwald. I didn't know where we were going. But we didn't eat. We didn't have no food. And my lips were black, and my teeth were loose. I remember shaking my teeth. And, and I remember I came to a town which is called Gera, in Germany already, because I saw a sign Gera. And the train stopped. And next to our train there was another train which was enclosed, but the door was slightly open. And I looked at the people in that train. They were frightening to look at them, because they were like dead, with their eyes sunken deep. They were Russian prisoners of war. And I looked at them. They didn't look human to me. They looked at us. We were in worse shape than they were, because we were in open train. But anyway, as the trains moved, people started to die. So we would take the bodies and just throw them overboard as the train moved. Some people jumped from the trains, and some of them were successful. Some of them were successful. They jumped from the train and they made it. Others were caught. Others were crippled, whatever. But the train kept on moving. And it came to a point, there were two brothers in the train, and one brother tells the other brother-- they've already been in camp. It's not use living. Let's die. An hour later, they were both dead, because the only thing that kept you alive was the hope of surviving.

speaker: interviewee
text: The will to live-- see, the, the-- we were at a point already, we were ready to give up. And once you give up that will, you're finished. And my good friend who came from the same village as I am-- Sam, he's in California today. And he kept on asking me, he said, Jack, why don't we die? And I said, listen, let's wait until tomorrow morning. And at night, it was cold. The rain kept-- and this is open trains. Came the morning, he said-- and he wanted to die already, you know? And when it came to it, I said, let's wait until tonight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the only thing I had to say, Sam, let's die, and chances are, I would've been dead, because really there was nothing, nothing to, to keep you alive. By the way, Elie Wiesel's father-- Elie Wiesel was on my train, my transport too. And his father died on that transport right after the-- right after the-- right after the transport went into Buchenwald, he was-- he was dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember when-- at night we came to city called Prague, in Prague, you know? We were staying like-- and the city was all blacked out. But one thing I want you to know, when we passed through Czechoslovakia-- I'm talking the Czechs, not the Slovaks. The Slovaks aren't the Czechs.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, for some reason, they must have called a station ahead, because there were civilians waiting for the trains over the overpasses. And they would throw-- and they would throw bread, and they would throw something into the train, the Czech people. And the Germans would shoot up into the air. And they would throw into the trains. The Czech people happened to be a very fine people. And apparently they knew that this train is coming, because they were waiting for us on the train. And we were hollering. And I don't-- I want you to know, I don't speak Polish. And we were hollering [POLISH]. In Polish it means, 'please throw us some snow.' Because it wasn't the food anymore. What we needed is water.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like boiling inside of you, you know? So you needed water. And snow-- I remember the snow started to fall, we would like, scrape each other and, and have some snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, certain things which are told-- which were told to me and I don't care, it could be 50 years, it's still inscribed in my mind. There was a-- going back to Buna-- there was a boy. He was from France. He was my age, but he was a very delicate boy. And he told me that he went to France, he was studying philosophy or something, I don't know, in France, in French. His father was a doctor. [SIGHS] He was in my bunk. And once there was an air raid. And he went out and he stole something from the kitchen to eat or whatever. And he was sentenced to die on the gallows. And when they took him out, that morning he told me, if you ever survive, look up my father. He lives in Rue [FRENCH]. And I don't speak-- I don't speak French. And I didn't have a pencil to write it down. But he told me Rue [FRENCH]. Whatever the street and the number. And I remember what he told me to this day. Well, of course, this was in Buna. And of course, the boy was hungry, you know. He was-- you know. I, by the way, looked up and I reported this. And then I found out the whole story about this boy, it's story by itself, what happened to this boy and how he came Auschwitz. It's a story by itself, but I'm just telling you. Even though I didn't speak the language, certain things I remember, because it was said to me and I'll never forget it. And so it was, I don't know, maybe 10, 12 days later. I don't remember. We came. The train stopped. And there were already people, skeletons. Some of them were dressed in the striped uniforms, and some of them had just plain civilian uniforms. They were already from Buchenwald. They were working around the camp there. And they took us off the train. And when we came off the train, we-- one had to support other. We were like-- nobody could walk anymore. You know, we had like, to support each other. With the hand. We were supporting, limping along. And they took us into the camp. By the camp, there is a building. And they gave me a new number. This number was no good. My number was like, 125,000-something, and I have the number downstairs. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they told us to go down. We're going to go for a shower. Now, I heard stories in Auschwitz. I was sure this is it, because-- and we had to walk even down to the steps, and that made it even more eerie. There were pipes alongside there, the staircase. And we were going down and then stairs up. And we went into a-- to a-- to a big room, big room. There were shower heads in the ceiling. And we're staying there. And I recited Ashrei, my prayer and I knew. Lo and behold, water is coming out, warm water. I open my mouth and I drank, you know, because you couldn't drink that water. It was all so contaminated or something. And we got a shower. And after we got a shower, they took-- they were outside. They brought a big tub. In the tub, there was kerosene. One guy got a hold of the head, other guy by your feet, and they dipped you into the kerosene for disinfecting, and that's it. And I was put into a camp, into the camp, into Buchenwald. See, Buchenwald had two-- it was one big camp, but there was one camp where there was Russian prisoners of war, Norwegian. You know, Buchenwald was a very-- it wasn't Jewish people. When I was there, most of the people were not Jewish. But behind the big camp, there was a little camp where the hospital was. It was called the kleine lager or the totenlager, the small camp or the death camp. In there, where most of the Jews were kept. So they didn't put me in the-- in the death lager, in the death camp. I was in the camp. And they put me to work in Buchenwald in the quarry, which is called a Steinbruch, the Steinbruch. The Steinbruch was a penal for people-- it was like a penal, penal colony. Very few people survived the Steinbruch, because what happened over there, they-- they took a big rock and they put it on you. And you had to carry it up. And when you weren't up, so somebody, an SS man, would push you. And then the rock would roll down and you [INAUDIBLE] fall down. The other guy would-- But when I was there, I met-- there was a guy who was a German civilian there who worked there. And he left me a piece of bread. And I worked in the Steinbruch not much, maybe about three, four days. And from there I was taken to work on the highway near Weimar, on the main Autobahn by Weimar, passing through Weimar. And when we went out, we went out at night, in the dark, because those days, I want you to know, this was already February, 1945, nothing in Germany moved during the day, the trains, the cars, even a horse. Because the planes, the American planes, the British planes, were always overhead and they would shoot up anything, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was working on the-- on the-- on the-- on the-- on the Autobahn, but I was already finished. I was finished. I, I couldn't walk any-- I mean, I had trouble walking. And I was just a skeleton, nothing, nothing left. So since I couldn't work anymore, so they took me and they put me into the death camp there, to the kleines lager. And I was in the-- in the small camp. And I didn't work anymore, but I was there. I think it was Block 66, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Block.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And one day, I bump into somebody. And he happens to come from next to my village. I told you there was a village with one Jewish family, [INAUDIBLE]. He was [INAUDIBLE]. He was from [INAUDIBLE]. And I thought that everybody's already dead from my village, because at this time already, I knew something happened. So I was very happy to see that somebody's alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE] says to me, he takes out a little book. It was a Tehillim. You know what Tehillim is? It's the-- it's a prayer book from the Psalms, Psalms, Psalms, a little-- I don't how he had it. He had it with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to me-- I was sitting there in the block. And he says to me, pray. So I picked the fourth [NON-ENGLISH], the fourth chapter of the Tehillim. And I started to say Tehillim.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I felt very good, because I didn't pray for almost a year now, you know? And it sort of brought back a little memories, you know. It was-- and the words were familiar. And I finished saying prayer. And I see-- and of course, you could hear artillery all around, you know? Planes overhead.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before this happened, before this happened, on the end of March, beginning of April, there was an announcement on the loudspeakers. The announcement said exactly, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. All Jewish prisoners should come to the appell, on the appell place, in an appell. A lot of them went up. They were taken out, shot or transported to other camps. Some of them went to Dachau. Some of them went to Theresienstadt. Because the Germans, even though they lost the war already, they didn't want to leave no Jews alive. And this was going on for a few days. And then I, I was forced on the appellplatz too. And they started to take them out from the appellplatz, so I lay down on the floor. And I played dead. I was dead. And there was a German officer, an SS guy, and he says, oh, this fellow is dead. So he took a match, put it in my lips, and he lit it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew that I'm not dead. I mean, you could see, you know? But I played dead. So as a joke, he wanted to show that I'm-- you know. And it burned. As a matter of fact, there was a picture which was taken after I was liberated from Buchenwald. For three months, I had a spot over here, a burn spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't move. But luckily, what happened, the very-- a minute, the same-- in the same minute, there was an air raid, the alarm went-- the, the, the air raid signals went up. And American planes were flying overhead, so all the Germans, they pulled out of the camp. And I ran back and I was sitting in a, in a, in a sewer. In a sewer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then coming out from the sewer, I met this [INAUDIBLE] from [INAUDIBLE], who gave me this Tehillim to say to pray, you know? And when I finished the prayers, I looked out the window. And there were huge tanks passing through, not in the camp, but below the camp. Those are American tanks. And we didn't know who it was, what it was, because I didn't even know America is at war. Somebody said, the British are here, the British are coming, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It reminds me of Paul Revere, you know? [LAUGHS] The British are coming. But we didn't know the Americans-- Americans at the war. And the tanks passed by. But for some reason, two hours later there was everything in camp. There were conserves, you know, meats, what you used to keep in, in, in-- it was called conserves, you know? In-- in-- in boxes. Butter, honey, marmalade, frankfurters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, I don't know, because what happened, the troops, when they passed by, they have to move on. They dumped everything out of the, of the, of the tanks, whatever they had, their supplies. And they were infiltered into the camps. So we all started to eat. That night, more people died from overeating and eating this rich food than they died during the German occupation. And the following morning, there were two American officers. They came through the gate of Buchenwald. And I want you to know something. Buchenwald, the streets were piled with dead, piled, like, like cork wood. And people outside even the-- who was alive couldn't move anymore. They couldn't walk. They couldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the two officers came through the camp and we came through the, the gate, they walked in through the Appellplatz. They started to get into the camp. They didn't say a word, but everybody was screaming, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't say-- they, they, they clammed up. I remember seeing their face. They were-- they didn't know it. They were like-- they just came up apparently on patrol or something. And they pulled right back. But the same day, the American army started to send in-- they were negro soldiers driving trucks with water and supplies. It was the first time I saw a negro man. And anyway, that was liberation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were liberated. And then every nationality started to send in, you know, for their people to come home. The French, they send in a whole-- the Red Cross and they send in armies with bands. And they took home their, their citizens and everybody. And if I would have waited for the Romanians to come and get me, I would still be in Buchenwald waiting for them. So I became a Czech citizen. I said, I'm a Czech Slovak citizen. And the Czechs sent in their trucks and they took me back to Czechoslovakia. And this is already the story after the war. After that, I went home and I came back to a DP camp and so on. From the DP camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, just mention what happened to you. Where were you in DP camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just briefly, what, what was that experience like? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You, you say you went home to Romania. After Buchenwald, we went home. We went home, and I-- but just I went back to Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always thought that I'm going to find my family alive. When I came back to my village in Comlausa, I went into the house where we used to live. And for the first time, for the first time, it hit me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mind you, when I lived through all this thing here, I never complained. I never cried, never-- you know? I took it like, in stride, you know, as it came along. But when I went into the house, it's like I saw everybody dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then I knew. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [DRINKS] Then I knew it's over. And it was only a question maybe somebody from the village who was alive. In my village, there were still about 14, 15 people who came back. Because when I came back, you know, there was no railroad station. I want you to know that the first time I rode a train was when the Nazis took me to Auschwitz. I was pretty happy that day, because I was riding a train. I come from a small village. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came home in my village and I heard Yiddish spoken, there was a man-- a boy and a girl. There we talking in the street, you know? And they spoke Yiddish. I thought, oh, maybe everybody's alive and they're surviving.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went into my house. My mother's sister was there. And I saw everything there, the furniture, everything. But I had a picture of all of them dead on the floor. It just suddenly-- it hit me like this. I walked out of the house. I walked out. All the Jewish people, we lived in one house in Comlausa. Then it started out with the Communists. You know, they-- the-- because we had some land over there and, and, and the Communists wanted to nationalize all the land and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So somebody told me, listen, best thing for you now is to leave here, leave Europe. So I remember going to the railroad. There was a little town called [NON-ENGLISH]. And the guy asked me, a ticket to where? I said, a ticket to New York, as a joke. And then I had to start all over again, go back to Satu Mare, start going through borders and coming back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I met up-- in Vienna I met up with a Berihah. Berihah was a-- was a-- like the, the underground from Palestine. Their, their, their job was to-- their job was to smuggle out all the Jews from Europe into Palestine. That was before there was a Jewish state. And from there, I was taken to Bergen-Belsen. And in Bergen-Belsen, I joined-- I stayed in Bergen-Belsen in a DP camp. And there I joined a Zionist foundation called Noam. Noam. Noam is called in Hebrew [HEBREW], The United Youth, United Youth. But Noam is also-- you know, after Tisha B'Av, after we read the Torah from Tisha B'Av, there is the [NON-ENGLISH], I mean, the [NON-ENGLISH]. It's called [NON-ENGLISH]. It's the first of Torah after Tisha B'Av, after the destruction of the Temple.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were called Noam and [NON-ENGLISH] is about the same thing. So we joined there. I remember Ben-Gurion coming there. It was in 1946. There was a conference in Basel and Ben-Gurion came.

speaker: interviewee
text: With a Zionist organization from Basel he came to Bergen-Belsen, because Basel was the first city where the first Zionist Congress was held and the last Zionist Congress was held. And Ben-Gurion came through. And I remember he made the remark that we are like ashes left over from the, from the, from the conflagration, you know, from Europe. And we didn't like that, so some of our people went up and told Ben-Gurion, listen, what you see is ashes, but behind those ashes are coal, burning coals. Don't ever call us ashes again. And-- And so he went to Basel. And I became very active in the Zionist movement. I joined-- there was a school there, a gymnasium. I went to the gymnasium But I didn't have no education, because my formal education is only, only three classes. And I became very active in the, in the movement there. And I was, I was trained as a-- to go to Palestine and be a-- you know, an officer in the, in the, in the Haganah. It was called Haganah those days. I still remember-- I still remember the commanding words and commander had to organize and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mind was always to come to America. Why? Because I thought to myself, if I go to America, from America I could go anywhere I want. If I don't like it here, I could go to Palestine. But once I go to Palestine, I don't know if I'll have the chance to come to America. My mind was always to come to America. So I pulled all kinds of tricks. I went to the American-- because I lived in the British zone. Bergen-Belsen was in the British zone. And there was a committee we just called American Committee for the Rescue of European Orphans. And Mrs. Roosevelt, she was the head of the committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to the consul and I made papers that I live in the American zone. And I came on April, 1947. And there was a troop ship called The left Bremerhaven also in April, 1947. And I came to New York. [LAUGHS] And this was a troop carrier. And when I came to New York, I got off. You know, we were all under 18, the whole boat, the whole ship was under 18. We were the European orphans. And when we came on the dock, there were women there in white dresses, you know, in white uniform. And they had cheese danishes and, and all kinds of danishes. And I came out and I saw those danish and I thought, it would be nice to buy something, but I didn't have no money. And after a while, one of the ladies says to me like this, I should go over.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I go over, she gives me one. She says, take more. So I took a few and I stuffed them into my shirt. So yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they came in buses to take us to our home. But we didn't want to leave, because the danishes were there, hot chocolate. Who-- I don't want to go any place.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to end there. This tape is over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
